summary:
Plato's Academy (c. 387–86 BC) was the philosophical school founded by Plato in a garden on the outskirts of Athens, widely considered the world's first institution of higher learning and the origin of the academic tradition that persists to the present day. Named for the grove of the hero Akademos where it was located, the Academy was a community of scholars dedicated to philosophical inquiry, mathematics, and political theory — organized around the shared pursuit of knowledge rather than professional instruction.

Plato founded the Academy following extensive travels after the death of Socrates, including visits to Pythagoreanism's center in southern Italy. The school attracted the finest minds in Greece; most notably the young Aristotle of Stagira, who studied there for twenty years before Plato's death and who would go on to found his own competing school, [[event:1026|Aristotle's Lyceum]]. The Academy's curriculum centered on mathematics — allegedly inscribed over the door was 'Let no one ignorant of geometry enter' — and on philosophical dialectic as the path to knowledge of the eternal Forms.

The Academy's political influence was significant: Plato was invited to Syracuse three times to tutor the Syracusan tyrant, in an ultimately frustrated attempt to create a philosopher-king. His major dialogues, composed throughout his life, circulated widely and defined the agenda of philosophical inquiry for subsequent centuries. When the Roman general Sulla sacked Athens in 86 BC, he reportedly had trees from the Academy's grove cut down for siege equipment, an event that symbolically marked the school's decline, though various successor schools carrying the Academy's name continued for centuries.

The Academy's intellectual legacy — particularly its commitment to systematic philosophical reasoning, the priority of mathematics, and the dialogue as a vehicle for inquiry — became one of the pillars of Western intellectual tradition.
